And I've seen people fall apart in that driver's seat, eyes glazed, words slurred and caught between rage and panic. Sometimes it's mental illness, sometimes it's drugs, and sometimes it's just someone who's reached the end of their rope. But to an officer, all those versions look the same at first, unstable and capable of doing something deadly in a second. When cops approach a car like this, they're not just walking into a conversation, they're stepping into a situation where the engine itself is a threat and every second counts. Breaking glass isn't about aggression, it's about survival. When someone's mentally gone but still gripping a steering wheel, that glass is the only thing between you and getting control of the suspect. Not every breakdown looks violent at first. And people may see this video and say why not wait him out? Well, because time isn't neutral. The longer you wait, the more that instability grows. Every cop I know has been there. One side of your brain wants to calm things down, the other screaming to act before someone gets hurt. So you're balancing compassion with command presence.
